I'd like to share the story of a young man who arrived at the hospital on August 5th  from Musanze, Northern Province. He is 14 years old, and he is suffering from skin disease  he is in the isolation room in ophthalmology he is with his mother.By the time they arrived at CHUK he received attentive care from the medical team, though the specialized diet essential for his recovery was lacking.
After advocating for him with the nutritionists, they promptly arranged for him to receive daily special diets, rich in protein from eggs and milk. Within two weeks, his wounds began to heal, enabling him to rise from his bed. He is now moving on his feet, and his now fit.he is expected to be  discharged this month.
His mother said that she had not hope for her child to recover but she is so grateful to SolidAfrica together with doctors for all care and  special diet which helped her child recover on he is on 80% all wounds are now healed because he has been given every day eggs, milk, fruits,no sugar porridge,bred and beef and fish meats body was extensively covered with wounds particularly his arms,chest and face, an legs and  he had 29kg, due to nutritious meals he now weighs 39kgs. Upon my encounter with him during my rounds with patients, we engaged in a meaningful conversation. He explained the circumstances of the disease. Following an examination at a nearby hospital, he was referred to CHUK for advanced treatment. During his time in the isolation room in ophthalmology, He is grateful for the assistance provided by Solid Africa during his hospitalization.

Today, we held the first meeting of 2025 for field officers at CHUK. During the meeting, we discussed the progress of our work and the challenges we are currently facing. Fortunately, the discussions were productive, and we found solutions to most of these challenges with ease.

One key issue raised was the frequent submission of medical leave by some employees—up to three times in a single month. To address this, we decided that only medical leave issued by government hospitals would be considered valid moving forward.

Another issue discussed was employees failing to report to work without notifying anyone of their absence. We agreed that in such cases, the employee would be allowed to return to work but only after being brought back by the authorities and having a discussion about the situation.

Overall, the meeting was constructive and provided clear steps for addressing our challenges effectively.

In 2021, CHUK received a 19-year-old patient weighing just 45 kg. His condition was critical, and he was immediately admitted to the Intensive Care Unit (ICU). As a field officer, I wasn’t allowed to enter the ICU, but I heard his story through the hospital’s nutritionist. This young man needed urgent help, as his family did not fully understand the severity of his condition.

For 10 long months, he remained in the ICU under intensive care. Following this period, he was transferred to the surgery ward, where he stayed for another year. It was during this time that we, in collaboration with Solid Africa, stepped in to provide support. Slowly, he began to recover and was able to eat again. We introduced him to a special diet rich in nutrients, including milk, eggs, and fruits, tailored to help him regain strength and weight.

After months of care and support, he showed remarkable improvement. By the time he was discharged, he had gained 8 kilograms and was on the path to full recovery. This young man expressed immense gratitude to Solid Africa for initiatives like Sukura, Kiza, and Gemura, which played a crucial role in his journey back to health.

He now reflects on his experience with deep appreciation, knowing that without the compassionate and comprehensive care provided by Solid Africa, his recovery might not have been possible. This story is a testament to the power of community-driven support and the life-changing impact it can have.

Nowadays, pineapple is being used in the diet for dialysis patients because passion fruit and tree tomato are not allowed. This change is primarily due to the high potassium content in passion fruit and tree tomato, which can be harmful to individuals on dialysis. Pineapple, on the other hand, is a low-potassium fruit, making it a safer alternative for managing potassium levels in these patients. Maintaining a proper diet with controlled potassium intake is essential for the health and well-being of those undergoing dialysis.

Today was a good day—our meals were well prepared, and our team had the chance to visit patients in the plastic surgery ward. Among them, we saw a young girl whose story has deeply touched our hearts.

Back in February, a 13-year-old girl was admitted to the hospital with severe burns that covered most of her body. From the very first day, the hospital nutritionist recommended a specialized diet to support her recovery: two eggs and two liters of milk every day. Unfortunately, her family was unable to afford this due to financial hardship.

Recognizing the urgency of her situation, the nutritionist reached out to us for support. From that moment on, we took her under our care—providing the nutrition and support she needed to heal. It has been a long and challenging journey, but through consistent care and compassion, she began to improve.

Today, we are happy to share that she has been discharged from the hospital. She is doing much better, and we are proud to have been part of her healing journey. This is why we do what we do because behind every meal is a life being changed.

Today, we encountered significant delays in providing lunch to patients. Unfortunately, this is not the first time such an issue has arisen.

Lunch service started around 15:00, which is far later than the scheduled time. Upon following up with the kitchen, the chef informed us that the delay was caused by a gas supply problem.

This situation negatively affected several patients, particularly those who rely on timely meals to take their medication. Such delays pose a risk to patient well-being and compromise the quality of care we aim to provide.

We recommend that this issue be addressed urgently and that preventive measures be put in place to ensure reliable meal preparation going forward.

Recommendations:

1. Investigate and resolve the recurring gas supply issues.


2. Establish a backup plan for meal preparation in case of future gas problems.

Report on Meeting with Social Worker Regarding Unauthorized Group Activity in Ward 7
Date: 6/05/2025
Location: chuk
 Hospital 

Purpose of the Meeting:
To discuss the situation concerning a group that has been coming to Ward 7 after feeding our dinner  to feed patients again without authorization.

Summary:
Today, we met with the hospital's social worker to address the issue of an external group that has been entering Ward 7 to feed patients after dinner. According to the social worker, this group had already been stopped from operating in the hospital since 2023.

The social worker informed us that the hospital is currently working in coordination with the security team and hospital administration to manage this matter. The group in question has been officially warned once again and instructed not to return to the hospital for this purpose.

It was emphasized that if the group continues to come back, it will be treated as a serious violation. The hospital has confirmed that no other group is allowed to feed patients except Solid Africa, which remains the only authorized organization for this service.

Conclusion:
We will continue to monitor the situation and work in collaboration with the hospital to ensure that policies are respected and patients' care is managed in a secure and coordinated manner.

Introduction:
This report highlights the recent decline in the number of children attending activities at Kina Ukire, compared to previous months.

Observation:
In recent weeks, there has been a noticeable drop in the number of children coming to Kina Ukire. Where we used to receive a larger group of children, we now often find only one or two attending.

Possible Causes:
One of the key contributing factors appears to be the lack of engaging materials and incentives. Previously, we provided toys, books, sweets, and biscuits, which played a major role in attracting children and motivating them to come early. Currently, due to limited resources, these items are no longer available, leading to reduced interest and participation among the children.

Conclusion:
The absence of small incentives and engaging materials has significantly impacted the attendance at Kina Ukire. Restoring access to such items could help bring back the children and improve participation in the program.

Recommendation:
We recommend sourcing or requesting support for items such as toys, storybooks, sweets, and snacks to help encourage children to return to Kina Ukire activities.

Background

For the past year, our team has been facing a persistent shortage of serving spoons required for daily patient meal distribution. Despite repeated requests, we have not been able to secure an adequate number of spoons to meet the needs of our operations.

Current Situation

The lack of sufficient serving spoons has resulted in continuous delays during mealtimes. Staff members are forced to wait for others to finish using the limited utensils before they can begin serving the next group of patients. This issue has significantly slowed down the meal service process and affected the overall quality of care provided to patients.

Impact

Delays in meal service, leading to extended waiting times for patients.

Increased staff fatigue due to inefficiencies in the serving process.

Reduced service quality, which compromises the patient experience.


Action Taken

Over the past year, multiple verbal and written requests have been made to address the shortage, but the issue remains unresolved.

Request

We are urgently requesting support to provide an adequate number of serving spoons to ensure timely and efficient meal distribution. Addressing this need will directly improve operational efficiency and patient care.

Conclusion

This is a recurring issue that requires immediate attention. We appreciate any assistance or donation of serving spoons to help us resolve this matter effectively.

For the past year, our team has been facing a persistent shortage of serving spoons required for daily patient meal distribution. Despite repeated requests, we have not been able to secure an adequate number of spoons to meet the needs of our operations.

Current Situation

The lack of sufficient serving spoons has resulted in continuous delays during mealtimes. Staff members are forced to wait for others to finish using the limited utensils before they can begin serving the next group of patients. This issue has significantly slowed down the meal service process and affected the overall quality of care provided to patients.

Impact

Delays in meal service, leading to extended waiting times for patients.

Increased staff fatigue due to inefficiencies in the serving process.

Reduced service quality, which compromises the patient experience.


Action Taken

Over the past year, multiple verbal and written requests have been made to address the shortage, but the issue remains unresolved.

Request

We are urgently requesting support to provide an adequate number of serving spoons to ensure timely and efficient meal distribution. Addressing this need will directly improve operational efficiency and patient care.

Today’s visit to all wards went well. During the discussions with patients in both the pediatric and adult emergency wards, several concerns were raised regarding supplies.

Key Findings:

Patients mentioned that they no longer receive mixed supplies as they used to in the past.

The main challenge highlighted was the lack of clothing. Patients explained that although they still receive other basic items, clothing is no longer provided.

This situation makes it difficult for them, especially after bathing, since they are forced to put back on the same clothes they had before.

Most patients arrive at the hospital without spare clothing, which increases their vulnerability.


Recommendation / Request:

Support with clothing for both pediatric and adult emergency patients, to improve their hygiene and dignity during hospitalization.

Conclusion
The fruit distribution was highly appreciated by the patients and caregivers. It demonstrated the value of continued support in improving patients’ nutrition and overall well,being.

Two days ago, we visited a mother in the neonatology ward. During our conversation, we discovered that she was experiencing deep sadness. She had delivered a premature baby at seven months, but at first, she did not have breast milk. This situation caused her emotional distress, especially because she had previously given birth eight times, and sadly, all the babies passed away shortly after birth.

The mother is also living with diabetes, which adds to her health challenges. After she shared her story with us, we offered her support and care. Thankfully, her condition has improved, and she has now started producing breast milk, allowing her to feed her baby.

This positive change has given her renewed hope and encouragement in caring for her newborn.

Today, we experienced a challenge in serving lunch and dinner due to a gas shortage, which caused delays in meal preparation. This issue occurs frequently whenever the gas supply runs out.

The delays had a significant impact on patients, especially those who must take their medication after eating. Some patients were forced to buy food  from outside, while other missed their medication doses because they could not afford to purchase meals.

We recommend that a sustainable solution be found to ensure consistent gas supply, as this issue directly affects patients' health and treatment adherence .
